retrieved_docs.title: Nitrous Oxide -- Continuing Education Activity
retrieved_docs.content: Nitrous oxide is an odorless, colorless, non-flammable gas. While nitrous oxide is not flammable, it will support combustion to the same extent as oxygen. It leads to a state of euphoria, explaining its nickname, ‘laughing gas.’ Nitrous oxide is the least potent inhalational anesthetic. Compared to other anesthetic agents, nitrous oxide causes minimal effects on respiration and hemodynamics. It cannot be a sole anesthetic agent and is often combined with a more potent and volatile anesthetic. The combination of analgesic and anesthetic effects makes nitrous oxide a valuable adjunct. This activity outlines the indications, mechanism of action, methods of administration, significant adverse effects, contraindications, monitoring, and toxicity of nitrous oxide, so providers can direct patient therapy to optimal outcomes in anesthesia and other conditions where nitrous oxide has therapeutic benefits.

retrieved_docs.title: Nitrous Oxide -- Review Questions
retrieved_docs.content: Access free multiple choice questions on this topic. Comment on this article.

retrieved_docs.title: Nitrous Oxide -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Nitrous oxide can be used for general anesthesia, procedural sedation, dental anesthesia, and to treat severe pain. Nitrous oxide's potent analgesic properties can be useful in providing analgesia in settings such as the obstetrical ward or emergency department. Its administration is often a 50% mixture of oxygen in these settings. Compared to other anesthetic agents, nitrous oxide causes minimal effects on respiration and hemodynamics. It leads to decreased tidal volume and increased respiratory rate but minimizes overall minute ventilation. Nitrous oxide leads to direct myocardial depression, but nitrous oxide's sympathetic stimulation reduces this effect, and the net effect is minimal. Unlike other volatile anesthetics, nitrous oxide has no muscle relaxation properties. [2] Nitrous oxide is also being investigated as a potential agent for treatment-resistant depression. However, further extensive research is needed. [3]

retrieved_docs.title: Nitrous Oxide -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: For general anesthesia, nitrous oxide(50 to 70%) is used. But due to its low potency, it can not be used as a single anesthetic agent; hence it is combined with other agents. Specially designated equipment for administering NO must be employed to ascertain concentrations of 50% NO and 50% oxygen. In contrast with dental apparatus, the device approved for obstetric use does not allow the clinician to modify the proportion of gases. [9]

retrieved_docs.title: Nitrous Oxide -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Nitrous oxide is an odorless, colorless, non-flammable gas. While nitrous oxide is not flammable, it will support combustion to the same extent as oxygen. It leads to a state of euphoria, explaining its nickname, 'laughing gas.' Nitrous oxide is the least potent inhalational anesthetic. Nitrous oxide requires a concentration of 104% to reach one minimum alveolar concentration (MAC). Thus it cannot be a sole anesthetic agent, and it is often combined with a more potent and volatile anesthetic. The combination of analgesic and anesthetic effects makes nitrous oxide a valuable adjunct. Nitrous oxide has a low blood solubility (blood-gas partition coefficient of 0.47), leading to a quick onset and offset. The low solubility leads to a concentrating effect for administered volatile agents in the lungs and is known as the second gas effect. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Nitrous Oxide -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: Induction: The combination of lower solubilities in blood and different tissues makes N2O one of the fastest anesthetic agents. N2O uptake in the lungs improves the blood concentrations of concomitantly administered other volatile inhalation agents and oxygen, leading to faster induction and improved arterial oxygenation.

retrieved_docs.title: Nitrous Oxide -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: Nitrous oxide administration is via inhalation utilizing a simple face mask, laryngeal mask airway, or an endotracheal tube. Administration of nitrous oxide according to the European Society of Anaesthesiology Task Force on Nitrous Oxide is given below. [7] For surgical procedural sedation and dental procedures, nitrous oxide (30 to 50%) is combined with oxygen. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthesia for Eye Surgery -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: Nitrous oxide should be avoided for 4 to 6 weeks after vitreoretinal surgery that uses an injected gas bubble. Nitrous oxide can enter and expand this gas bubble causing ischemia of the optic nerve.

retrieved_docs.title: Anesthesia Machine -- Clinical Significance
retrieved_docs.content: The modern anesthesia machine implementing a closed-circuit system allows for recycling inhaled gases (i.e., oxygen, nitrous oxide, isoflurane, sevoflurane, etc.), resulting in a significant reduction in environmental pollution.  Advances in carbon dioxide absorbents have allowed carbon dioxide to be absorbed and filtered out from exhaled gases so that oxygen and volatile anesthetics can be re-used.  The effectiveness of recycling gases can be compounded by utilizing the practice of low-flow anesthesia (gas flows less than alveolar ventilation).  Anesthesia circuits without a closed-circuit system may be used to administer anesthesia; however, at a significant financial cost to administer anesthetic and significant environmental impact when implemented widely. [4]

retrieved_docs.title: Nitrous Oxide -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: Maintenance: N2O is mixed with different drugs during maintenance because of its insufficient anesthetic potency. As discussed above, nitrous oxide has a MAC of 105%, but the provision of sufficient oxygen delivery precludes the administration of concentrations above 70–75%, thus limiting its use to 0.7 MAC. Combining propofol with nitrous oxide for dental sedation decreases propofol requirements and reduces the hypotensive effects compared to propofol alone.

retrieved_docs.title: Inhalational Anesthetic -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: Inhalation anesthetics (nitrous oxide, halothane, isoflurane, desflurane, sevoflurane, most commonly used agents in practice today) are used for induction and maintenance of general anesthesia in the operating room. The volatile anesthetics (halothane, isoflurane, desflurane, and sevoflurane) are liquids at room temperature and require the use of vaporizers for inhalational administration. Nitrous Oxide is already under normal conditions of temperature and pressure. All inhalational anesthetics provide amnesia and immobility, except for nitrous oxide, which also provides analgesia. Inhaled anesthetics are commonly used in combination with IV anesthetic agents. These agents have FDA approval for use as a general anesthetic and sedation agent in the operating room. Inhaled anesthetic agents have also had use in the intensive care unit, but this is not an FDA-approved indication. The primary applications of inhaled anesthetic agents in the ICU are sedation, refractory bronchospasm, and control of status epilepticus unresponsive to anticonvulsant medications.

retrieved_docs.title: Nitrous Oxide -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Many contraindications to nitrous use are relative and may vary based on the provider. These include:

retrieved_docs.title: Nitrous Oxide -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: Emergence: Nitrous oxide quickens emergence from anesthesia. In addition, nitrous oxide has a short elimination half-time; hence washout from the brain is swift because of its lower lipid solubility, leading to rapid recovery. [8]

retrieved_docs.title: Nitrous Oxide -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Impaired consciousness [7]

retrieved_docs.title: Inhalational Anesthetic -- Contraindications
retrieved_docs.content: Nitrous oxide is contraindicated in patients undergoing craniotomies, bowel surgery, intraocular and middle ear surgeries. Nitrous oxide is thirty times more soluble than nitrogen. This leads to the rapid removal of nitrogen in these closed spaces. Nitrous oxide will quickly diffuse into these closed spaces leading to increased pressure/volume in the brain, bowel, eye, and middle ear. In addition, patients with pneumothorax or pulmonary hypertension can have worsening of the pneumothorax and increases in pulmonary hypertension related to the use of nitrous oxide.

retrieved_docs.title: Inhalational Anesthetic -- Administration
retrieved_docs.content: Nitrous oxide, also known as "laughing gas," is a non-flammable, odorless, and colorless gas. It has a MAC of 104%. Unlike other inhaled anesthetics currently used, it has analgesic properties. Its use has fallen more into favor as an anesthetic option for laboring patients in the obstetric wards.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 3 -- Level 3 -- Community 1324
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics encompass a diverse group of agents that are used to induce a reversible loss of consciousness for surgical and diagnostic procedures. The class of general anesthetics includes chloroform, enfluran, fencyklidin, flurothyl, methohexital, methoxyfluran, oxid dusný (nitrous oxide), and propofol. Each of these agents has distinct pharmacologic characteristics, such as differences in onset speed, duration of action, potency, and side‑effect profile, which influence their selection for specific clinical scenarios. Understanding the full spectrum of general anesthetic agents—ranging from the historic inhalational agent chloroform to the widely used intravenous agent propofol—allows clinicians to tailor anesthetic plans to individual patient needs, procedural requirements, and safety considerations, thereby optimizing peri‑operative care and outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 3 -- Community 2045
retrieved_docs.content: Anesthetics, general constitute a broad class of agents used to produce reversible loss of consciousness and analgesia for surgical procedures. This class includes chloroform, enfluran, fencyklidin, flurothyl, isofluran, ketamin, methohexital, methoxyfluran, oxid dusný (nitrous oxide), and propofol, each of which is a specific child of the general anesthetic category. In addition, xenon is classified as a type of general anesthetic. Together, these diverse compounds—ranging from volatile liquids such as chloroform and isofluran to intravenous agents like propofol and ketamin, and to gaseous agents such as nitrous oxide and xenon—provide clinicians with multiple options for tailoring anesthetic care to the physiological needs, safety considerations, and procedural requirements of individual patients. Understanding the relationships among these agents supports informed selection, dosing, and monitoring strategies in peri‑operative medicine.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 2 -- Level 3 -- Community 1638
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics constitute a diverse group of agents used to produce reversible loss of consciousness and analgesia for surgical procedures. The category “anesthetics, general” includes chloroform, enflurane, fencyclidine, flurothyl, isoflurane, methohexital, and propofol as child agents, each representing a distinct chemical class with specific pharmacodynamic and pharmacokinetic profiles. In addition, xenon—a noble gas—also falls under the classification of general anesthetics, indicating that it shares the core functional purpose of inducing general anesthesia despite its unique mechanism of action. Together, these agents illustrate the breadth of substances that can be employed to achieve controlled, reversible unconsciousness in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 844
retrieved_docs.content: Procedures involving the use of pharmaceuticals encompass a range of therapeutic interventions, and a specific sub‑procedure within this category is the administration of inhaled nitric oxide, which is considered a child procedure of the broader pharmaceutical‑based procedural group.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 3 -- Community 1314
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics encompass a broad spectrum of agents that are classified under the category “anesthetics, general.” This group includes the inhalational agents chloroform, enfluran, fencyklidin, and flurothyl, as well as the intravenous agents methohexital, oxid dusný, and propofol. In addition, xenon is identified as a specific type of general anesthetic. Together, these substances represent the diverse pharmacologic options used to achieve reversible loss of consciousness and analgesia during surgical and procedural care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 20 -- Level 2 -- Community 925
retrieved_docs.content: The community describes GABA modulátory as drugs that act on the gamma‑aminobutyric acid system and frequently overlap with both hypnotics‑sedatives and general anesthetics, reflecting shared mechanisms of sedation, hypnosis, and anesthesia. Within the hypnotics‑sedatives class, chloralosa, etomidát, flurazepam, hexobarbital and halothan are listed, and the subgroup “látky navozující spánek” (sleep‑inducing substances) is included. The general anesthetics class, defined as agents that produce a reversible loss of consciousness, comprises chloralosa, chloroform, enfluran, etomidát, fencyklidin, flurothyl, halothan, hexobarbital, isofluran (isoflurane), methohexital, oxid‑dusný (nitrous oxide), propofol, and the noble gas xenon; the Arabic term التخدير العام denotes this category. Flurazepam is highlighted as a benzodiazepine GABA modulator that enhances GABA‑mediated inhibition. Additionally, ethany is linked to isofluran, indicating a specific relationship with the volatile inhalational anesthetic isoflurane. The integrated framework emphasizes the pharmacologic intersections among GABA‑modulating agents, sleep‑inducing hypnotics, and diverse general anesthetics, supporting informed anesthesia planning and patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 3 -- Community 1477
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics constitute a broad class of agents used to induce reversible loss of consciousness, analgesia, amnesia, and muscle relaxation for surgical procedures. Within this class, chloroform, enfluran (enflurane), fencyklidin, flurothyl, isofluran (isoflurane), methohexital, oxid dusný, and propofol are recognized as specific child agents that share the overarching function of producing general anesthesia. Xenon is also classified as a type of general anesthetic, reflecting its role as an inhalational agent with anesthetic properties. Although these agents differ in chemical structure, potency, onset and recovery times, and side‑effect profiles, they are all integrated into the therapeutic repertoire for achieving controlled, reversible unconsciousness in patients undergoing operative or diagnostic interventions. Understanding the relationships among these individual anesthetic agents and the general anesthetic category supports informed selection, dosing, and monitoring strategies to optimize patient safety and perioperative care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 4 -- Community 2718
retrieved_docs.content: Methoxyfluran, a halogenated ether anesthetic, is recognized for its ability to induce فقدان الوعي (loss of consciousness), which is a central therapeutic objective in the administration of general anesthesia. By depressing central nervous system activity, methoxyfluran reliably produces the unconscious state required for surgical procedures, highlighting its role as an effective agent for achieving controlled loss of consciousness in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 4 -- Level 2 -- Community 394
retrieved_docs.content: GABA modulators are characterized as antagonists and inhibitory agents that can block or diminish GABA‑mediated neurotransmission. This pharmacologic profile encompasses a broad spectrum of compounds, including the volatile anesthetic gases chloroform, enflurane, halothane, isoflurane and nitrous oxide; the intravenous anesthetics etomidate, methohexital, propofol and bupivacaine; the barbiturates flurazepam and flurothyl; the dissociative agent fencyclidine; the hypnotic‑sedative chloralose; the carboxylic acids; the inert gas nitrous oxide; the mineral kaolin; as well as amitriptyline, thiorphan and other hypnotic‑sedative agents that are listed as allowed qualifiers for antagonist‑inhibitory actions. Recognizing these relationships aids clinicians in selecting drugs for anesthesia, sedation, analgesia and neuropsychiatric treatment and in anticipating therapeutic effects, drug interactions and adverse outcomes. Antagonists and inhibitors are frequently qualified by local‑anesthetic

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 11 -- Level 2 -- Community 348
retrieved_docs.content: GABA modulátory constitute a pharmacologic class that modulates the gamma‑aminobutyric acid (GABA) system and includes agents such as flurazepam and flurazepam‑related compounds; this class overlaps with the hypnotics‑and‑sedatives category, which comprises chloralosa, etomidát, flurazepam, halothan, hexobarbital, methapyrilen and the sub‑class “látky navozující spánek” (sleep‑inducing substances). Many of these hypnotic‑sedative agents—specifically chloralosa, etomidát, halothan and hexobarbital—are also classified as general anesthetics, a broad group that contains chloralosa, chloroform, enfluran, etomidát, fencyklidin, flurothyl, halothan, hexobarbital, isofluran, methohexital, oxid dusný (nitrous oxide) and others. Within the general anesthetic class, propofol is identified as a child agent representing a widely used intravenous anesthetic, while xenon, a noble gas, and the volatile inhalational agents isoflurane (linked to the entity ethany) and isofluran exemplify inhalational options. The interconnections among GABA modulátory, hypnotics‑and‑sedatives, and general anesthetics—highlighted by agents that belong to multiple categories—underscore the shared pharmacologic mechanisms that produce sedation, hypnosis, and loss of consciousness, informing therapeutic choices and patient‑care strategies in anesthesia and related clinical settings.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 1416
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics constitute a diverse class of pharmacologic agents used to induce a reversible loss of consciousness, analgesia, amnesia, and muscle relaxation for surgical procedures. This class includes chloralosa, chloroform, enfluran, etomidát, fencyklidin, flurothyl, isofluran, ketamin, methohexital, oxid dusný, and propofol. Each of these agents functions as a “child” of the broader category of general anesthetics, reflecting their role as specific drugs that share the common purpose of producing and maintaining general anesthesia. While all serve the same overarching clinical goal, they differ in chemical structure, mechanism of action, potency, onset and duration of effect, and side‑effect profile, allowing clinicians to tailor anesthetic plans to the individual patient’s physiological status, surgical requirements, and risk factors. Understanding the full spectrum of these agents enhances patient safety, optimizes peri‑operative management, and supports informed selection of the most appropriate anesthetic regimen.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 12 -- Level 2 -- Community 460
retrieved_docs.content: GABA‑modulating agents are linked to hypnotic, sedative, and general anesthetic effects, indicating that drugs acting on the GABAergic system can produce sedation, hypnosis, and reversible loss of consciousness. Specific compounds classified as hypnotics and sedatives—including the combination aspirin / butalbital / caffeine, chloralose, etomidate, flurazepam, hexobarbital, and halothane—belong to the broader parent class of CNS‑depressant substances (“látky tlumící činnost CNS”). General anesthetics, a subset of CNS depressants, comprise a diverse group of inhalational and intravenous agents such as chloralose, chloroform, enflurane, etomidate, fencyclidine, flurothyl, halothane, hexobarbital, methohexital, nitrous oxide, propofol, and the noble gas xenon. Xenon exemplifies how chemically distinct agents can function as reversible general anesthetics, while flurazepam exemplifies a GABA modulator that enhances inhibitory neurotransmission. Together, these relationships illustrate the overlapping pharmacologic landscape in which GABA‑modulating hypnotics/sedatives and a wide array of general anesthetics intersect within CNS‑depressant therapy and peri‑operative patient care.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2055
retrieved_docs.content: Halothane is contraindicated for use in patients receiving metaraminol, indicating that the inhalational anesthetic should be avoided when the vasopressor metaraminol is administered due to potential adverse interactions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 2243
retrieved_docs.content: General anesthetics constitute a class of drugs used to induce a reversible loss of consciousness for surgical procedures, and they are collectively referred to in Arabic as “التخدير العام.” This category includes a diverse group of agents that are considered “children” of the general anesthetic class, such as chloroform, enflurane, fencyclidine, flurothyl, isoflurane, methohexital, oxid dusný, and propofol. In addition, xenon is classified as a type of general anesthetic, illustrating that both traditional volatile compounds (e.g., chloroform, enflurane, isoflurane) and newer agents (e.g., propofol, methohexital, xenon) belong to the same therapeutic group. Understanding that these substances share the common purpose of producing general anesthesia helps clinicians select appropriate agents based on pharmacologic properties, safety profiles, and specific patient needs.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 9 -- Level 3 -- Community 1691
retrieved_docs.content: Respiratory intubation and mechanical ventilation is recognized as both the specific medical procedure and the category that defines it, effectively classifying itself within the domain of airway management and ventilatory support.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 13 -- Level 3 -- Community 1357
retrieved_docs.content: Fenazon and kokain are both classified as members of the broader category of analgesics and anesthetics, indicating that each of these agents is used for pain relief and for inducing anesthesia. As part of this pharmacologic class, they share the common purpose of managing pain and facilitating surgical or procedural sedation, and their inclusion in the analgesics and anesthetics group underscores their relevance to therapeutic strategies aimed at controlling nociception and providing anesthetic effects.
